does: This paper introduces Alpha-Divergence Preference Optimization (APO), a framework that unifies forward and reverse KL divergence behavior through Csiszár alpha-divergence within an anchored geometric setting. APO parameterizes a continuous spectrum of divergence behaviors controlled by a scalar parameter alpha, enabling smooth interpolation between mode-covering (forward KL) and mode-seeking (reverse KL) updates during preference optimization. The authors derive the unified gradient dynamics for this family, analyze variance properties across the divergence spectrum, and propose a practical reward-and-confidence-guarded schedule that automatically transitions from coverage-focused to exploitation-focused behavior as training progresses and reward estimates stabilize.
connects: This work directly extends recent anchored preference optimization methods like ADPO that improve stability by performing KL projections in anchored coordinates rather than standard parameter space. It responds to a fundamental tension in alignment practice: forward KL divergence used in supervised fine-tuning achieves stability but leaves high-reward modes underexploited, while reverse KL in PPO-style RLHF enables exploitation but risks mode collapse and training instability. The paper builds on decades of divergence measure research in information theory and connects to the broader literature on interpolating between KL variants in variational inference and reinforcement learning, while positioning itself as a more general framework than methods committing to single divergence regimes.
enables: APO opens directions for more flexible alignment training by treating divergence choice as a learnable hyperparameter rather than a fixed architectural commitment. The framework enables adaptive training strategies that start conservative and coverage-focused before transitioning to aggressive reward-seeking, potentially improving sample efficiency and final performance simultaneously. Future work can develop better adaptive schedules, apply alpha-divergence interpolation to other preference learning objectives beyond KL constraints, and investigate how this perspective illuminates the theoretical foundations of successful alignment methods. The unified gradient analysis could inform principled algorithm design for other divergence-based objectives and provide tools for understanding stability-exploitation tradeoffs in human feedback learning more broadly.
